title: The kettle at the repair café
summary: At a Saturday repair café in Amsterdam, a volunteer and a woman with a dead electric kettle talk through whether it is worth fixing — and what 'worth it' really means once you count more than money.

body.speaker: Sanne
body.text: Good morning. Bring it to the bench. So — the kettle has stopped heating. Tell me what happened before it died.

body.speaker: Margriet
body.text: Nothing dramatic. One morning it just clicked and stayed cold. I almost threw it out, but my granddaughter said there was a place that fixes things for free, so here I am. Honestly, I feel a little silly. A new one is only twenty euros.

body.speaker: Sanne
body.text: You are not silly. But let me be honest with you too, because I am not here to lie to keep you happy. Sometimes the new one really is the better choice, and I will tell you if it is. Let me open it first.

body.speaker: Margriet
body.text: You would tell me to buy a new one? At a repair café?

body.speaker: Sanne
body.text: Of course. Repair is not a religion. If the heating element is burnt through and the part costs eighteen euros and an hour of work, then a twenty-euro kettle is the sensible answer, and pretending otherwise helps no one. Ah — look. It is not the element. It is this little switch. A two-euro part.

body.speaker: Sanne
body.text: In money, clearly yes. But I have stopped thinking about it only in money, and I will tell you why. The kettle you nearly threw away is made of perhaps forty parts. Thirty-nine of them are perfect. When you buy a new kettle, you are not replacing one broken switch. You are throwing away thirty-nine working parts to avoid changing one.

body.speaker: Margriet
body.text: I had never thought of it that way. But surely one kettle is nothing.

body.speaker: Sanne
body.text: One kettle is nothing. You are right, and I will not pretend it saves the world. But the habit is not nothing. The person who learns that a dead kettle can wake up looks at the next dead thing differently — the toaster, the lamp, the bicycle. The repair is small. The change in how you see your own belongings is not small.

body.speaker: Margriet
body.text: My husband would have fixed this himself. He could mend anything. After he died I think I just... stopped believing things could be mended.

body.speaker: Sanne
body.text: Then this is a good kettle to start with. Here, take the screwdriver. No — your hands, not mine. I will tell you where to turn. You will not learn anything watching me, and you will not believe it is fixed unless you feel it click back into place yourself.

body.speaker: Margriet
body.text: And if I break it further?

body.speaker: Sanne
body.text: Then it was going to the bin anyway, so you have lost nothing and learned something. That is the quiet secret of this whole room. Nobody here is afraid of breaking what is already broken. Turn it slowly. There. Now you have not just a kettle. You have the small, dangerous idea that the next broken thing is also yours to try.

annotations.meaningVi: Sửa chữa không phải một thứ tín ngưỡng
những bộ phận còn hoạt động
Ông ấy sửa được mọi thứ
dù sao cũng sắp bị vứt thùng rác
cái ý nghĩ nhỏ mà nguy hiểm

grammarNotes.title: Third conditional + present perfect for a changed habit
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 để nói về quá khứ giả định: 'My husband would have fixed this himself.' (would have + V3). Bài cũng dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn tả một thay đổi kéo dài đến hiện tại: 'I have stopped thinking about it only in money.' / 'After he died I think I just... stopped believing things could be mended.' — chỉ một trạng thái bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn đúng đến giờ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The text uses the third conditional for the hypothetical past: 'My husband would have fixed this himself.' (would have + V3). It also uses the present perfect for a change continuing to now: 'I have stopped thinking about it only in money.' / 'I just... stopped believing things could be mended.' — a state that began in the past and still holds.

translationVi:
Sanne: Chào buổi sáng. Mang nó lên bàn đi bà. Vậy là — cái ấm đã ngừng đun nóng. Kể cháu nghe chuyện gì xảy ra trước khi nó hỏng.

Margriet: Chẳng có gì kịch tính. Một buổi sáng nó kêu tách rồi cứ thế nguội ngắt. Tôi suýt vứt đi, nhưng cháu gái tôi bảo có một nơi sửa đồ miễn phí, nên tôi đến đây. Thật tình tôi thấy hơi ngớ ngẩn. Cái mới chỉ hai mươi euro.

Sanne: Bà không ngớ ngẩn đâu. Nhưng cháu cũng nói thật với bà, vì cháu không ở đây để nói dối cho bà vui. Đôi khi cái mới thật sự là lựa chọn tốt hơn, và cháu sẽ nói nếu đúng vậy. Để cháu mở nó ra trước.

Margriet: Cháu sẽ bảo tôi đi mua cái mới à? Tại một quán sửa đồ?

Sanne: Tất nhiên. Sửa chữa không phải một thứ tín ngưỡng. Nếu bộ phận làm nóng cháy đứt và linh kiện tốn mười tám euro cộng một giờ công, thì cái ấm hai mươi euro là câu trả lời hợp lý, và giả vờ ngược lại chẳng giúp ai. À — nhìn này. Không phải bộ làm nóng. Là cái công tắc nhỏ này. Linh kiện hai euro.

Margriet: Vậy là đáng sửa.

Sanne: Về tiền thì rõ là đáng. Nhưng cháu đã thôi nghĩ về nó chỉ bằng tiền, và cháu sẽ nói bà nghe vì sao. Cái ấm bà suýt vứt được làm từ chừng bốn mươi bộ phận. Ba mươi chín cái còn hoàn hảo. Khi bà mua một cái ấm mới, bà không phải thay một cái công tắc hỏng. Bà đang vứt đi ba mươi chín bộ phận còn tốt chỉ để khỏi phải thay một cái.

Margriet: Tôi chưa bao giờ nghĩ theo cách đó. Nhưng chắc chắn một cái ấm thì có là gì.

Sanne: Một cái ấm thì chẳng là gì. Bà nói đúng, và cháu sẽ không vờ rằng nó cứu thế giới. Nhưng cái thói quen thì không phải chẳng là gì. Người học được rằng một cái ấm 'chết' có thể sống lại sẽ nhìn thứ hỏng tiếp theo bằng con mắt khác — cái lò nướng, cái đèn, cái xe đạp. Việc sửa thì nhỏ. Sự thay đổi trong cách bà nhìn đồ đạc của mình thì không nhỏ.

Margriet: Chồng tôi hẳn đã tự sửa cái này. Ông ấy sửa được mọi thứ. Sau khi ông mất tôi nghĩ mình chỉ... thôi không còn tin rằng đồ vật có thể sửa được.

Sanne: Vậy thì đây là một cái ấm tốt để bắt đầu. Đây, cầm tua vít đi. Không — tay bà, không phải tay cháu. Cháu sẽ chỉ bà chỗ nào để vặn. Bà sẽ không học được gì khi nhìn cháu làm, và bà sẽ không tin nó đã sửa xong trừ khi bà tự tay cảm thấy nó khớp trở lại đúng chỗ.

Margriet: Lỡ tôi làm hỏng thêm thì sao?

Sanne: Thì dù sao nó cũng sắp ra thùng rác, nên bà chẳng mất gì mà lại học được điều gì đó. Đó là bí mật âm thầm của cả căn phòng này. Ở đây không ai sợ làm hỏng cái vốn đã hỏng. Vặn từ từ. Đó. Giờ bà không chỉ có một cái ấm. Bà có một ý nghĩ nhỏ mà nguy hiểm: rằng thứ hỏng tiếp theo cũng là của bà để thử.

questions.prompt: Why does Margriet feel a little silly bringing the kettle in?
options: A new kettle costs only twenty euros
She does not own a kettle
questions.explanation: 'I feel a little silly. A new one is only twenty euros.'

questions.prompt: What does Sanne say she will honestly do if buying new is better?
options: Tell Margriet to buy a new one — repair is not a religion
Hide the truth to keep her happy
questions.explanation: 'Repair is not a religion... I will tell you if it is.'

questions.prompt: What turns out to be wrong with the kettle?
options: A little switch — a two-euro part, not the heating element
questions.explanation: 'It is not the element. It is this little switch. A two-euro part.'

questions.prompt: Beyond money, how does Sanne reframe buying a new kettle?
options: You throw away thirty-nine working parts to avoid changing one
You save more money long term
questions.explanation: 'You are throwing away thirty-nine working parts to avoid changing one.'

questions.prompt: Does Sanne claim fixing one kettle saves the world?
options: No — she agrees one kettle is nothing, but says the habit is not nothing
She says it harms the environment
questions.explanation: 'One kettle is nothing... But the habit is not nothing.'

questions.prompt: What does Sanne say the repair really changes?
options: How you see your own belongings — you look at the next broken thing differently
questions.explanation: 'The change in how you see your own belongings is not small.'

questions.prompt: What does Margriet reveal about her husband?
options: He could mend anything, and after he died she stopped believing things could be mended
He bought a new kettle every year
questions.explanation: 'He could mend anything. After he died I think I just... stopped believing things could be mended.'

questions.prompt: Why does Sanne insist Margriet use the screwdriver herself?
options: She will not learn or believe it is fixed unless she feels it click into place herself
questions.explanation: 'you will not believe it is fixed unless you feel it click back into place yourself.'

questions.prompt: What does Sanne say if Margriet breaks it further?
options: It was going to the bin anyway, so she has lost nothing and learned something
She must pay for a replacement
She will be asked to leave
questions.explanation: 'Then it was going to the bin anyway, so you have lost nothing and learned something.'

questions.prompt: What 'quiet secret' of the room does Sanne describe?
options: Nobody here is afraid of breaking what is already broken
Volunteers do all the work themselves
questions.explanation: 'Nobody here is afraid of breaking what is already broken.'

questions.prompt: What is 'the small, dangerous idea' Margriet leaves with?
options: That the next broken thing is also hers to try
That she should sell her old things
questions.explanation: 'the small, dangerous idea that the next broken thing is also yours to try.'

options: Repair is worth it less for any single object than for the habit and confidence it builds — once you mend one dead thing yourself, you start to see everything you own as fixable.
Repair cafés should refuse cheap items.
Old people cannot learn to repair things.
questions.explanation: The dialogue centres on the habit and change of mindset, not the kettle alone.

Margriet: Nothing dramatic. One morning it just clicked and stayed cold. I almost threw it out, but my granddaughter said there was a place that fixes things for free, so here I am. Honestly, I feel a little {{b2}}. A new one is only twenty euros.
Sanne: You are not silly. But let me be honest with you too, because I am not here to lie to keep you happy. Sometimes the new one really is the better choice, and I will tell you if it is. Let me open it first.
Margriet: You would tell me to buy a new one? At a repair café?
Sanne: Of course. Repair is not a {{b3}}. If the heating element is burnt through and the part costs eighteen euros and an hour of work, then a twenty-euro kettle is the sensible answer. Ah — look. It is not the element. It is this little {{b4}}. A two-euro part.
Margriet: So it is worth fixing.
Sanne: In money, clearly yes. But I have stopped thinking about it only in {{b5}}. The kettle you nearly threw away is made of perhaps forty parts. Thirty-nine of them are perfect. When you buy a new kettle, you are throwing away thirty-nine {{b6}} parts to avoid changing one.
Margriet: I had never thought of it that way. But surely one kettle is nothing.
Sanne: One kettle is nothing. You are right, and I will not pretend it saves the world. But the {{b7}} is not nothing. The person who learns that a dead kettle can wake up looks at the next dead thing differently — the toaster, the lamp, the bicycle. The repair is small. The change in how you see your own {{b8}} is not small.
Margriet: My husband would have fixed this himself. He could mend anything. After he died I think I just... stopped believing things could be {{b9}}.
Sanne: Then this is a good kettle to start with. Here, take the screwdriver. No — your hands, not mine. You will not believe it is fixed unless you feel it click back into place yourself.
Margriet: And if I break it further?
Sanne: Then it was going to the {{b10}} anyway, so you have lost nothing and learned something. That is the quiet secret of this whole room. Nobody here is afraid of breaking what is already broken. Turn it slowly. There. Now you have not just a kettle. You have the small, dangerous {{b11}} that the next broken thing is also yours to try.

blanks.explanation: How you see your own belongings.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Sanne argues that the real value of a repair is the change in how you see your belongings, not the saved money. Is there something you discarded recently that you could have fixed — and what stopped you: the cost, the time, or the belief that it could not be done?
